ac:rich-text-body.p: A successful SKA proposal articulates compelling science, specifies technical requirements, and provides all necessary metadata. This page describes the key attributes and information needed in every proposal.

p: Every SKA proposal must include the following attributes and information:

ul.li: Proposal Title: A concise, descriptive title (typically <100 characters) conveying the primary science goal

Abstract: A 200–500 word summary for non-expert readers. Must clearly convey:
The scientific question being addressed
Why the SKA is essential for answering it
Expected outcomes and impact
Accessibility to broad audience (avoid heavy jargon)

p: All proposals are assigned to a primary science category (and optionally secondary categories). Categories include:

ul.li: Cosmology & Large-Scale Structure — HI surveys, dark energy, reionization, galaxy evolution
Compact Objects — Pulsars, black holes, neutron stars, magnetars
Active Galaxies & High-Energy Astrophysics — AGN, jets, X-ray binaries, TDE
Stars & Star Formation — Stellar masers, star-forming regions, protoplanetary disks
Transient & Time-Domain Astronomy — Supernovae, GRBs, FRBs, novae
Galactic & Extragalactic Surveys — Source catalogs, sky surveys
Solar & Ionospheric Physics — Solar radio bursts, space weather
Fundamental Physics & Cosmology — Pulsar timing arrays, gravitational waves, relativity tests
Techniques & Surveys — New observing modes, commissioning science, pilot surveys
Name and affiliation: Full details of lead scientist
Contact information: Email and telephone for proposal correspondence
Role statement: Brief description of PI's expertise and role in the project
Full list: All team members contributing to the science or observations
Affiliations: Institutional home of each co-investigator
Role descriptions: Specific contributions (e.g., "data analysis," "theoretical modeling," "target selection")
Contact representatives: Designated contacts for specific aspects (e.g., correlator requirements, data processing)
Collaboration name: If part of a formal science collaboration or consortium
Governance structure: How decisions are made, publication rules, proprietary period
Co-investigator list: May be very large; summary statistics and organizational structure should be provided
SKA-Low: Frequency range 50–350 MHz, primarily for HI and low-frequency spectral-line science
SKA-Mid: Frequency range 0.35–15.4 GHz, for continuum, high-frequency lines, VLBI, pulsars
Both telescopes: If science requires multi-frequency observations
Frequency band(s): Specific frequency ranges within SKA-Low or SKA-Mid (e.g., "1.4 GHz band" for SKA-Mid)
Bandwidth: Continuous bandwidth required (e.g., "Full 400 MHz bandwidth" or "Limited to 100 MHz")
Observing mode: Continuum imaging, spectral-line, VLBI, pulsar search/timing, commensal, etc.
Frequency switching or multi-band: If simultaneous observations in multiple bands needed
Total observing time: Requested time on sky (in hours), separate for each telescope if multi-telescope proposal
Justification: Sensitivity calculations or survey considerations explaining time requirement
Time flexibility: Whether time is flexible (can be split across multiple observing runs) or must be contiguous
Scheduling constraints: Any required timing (e.g., "must observe during new moon," "must include both summer and winter observations")
Requested subarray size: Full array, large subarray, medium, small, or flexible
Flexibility: Whether proposal is robust to smaller-than-requested subarray (important for scheduling)
Specific dishes/stations: If particular array elements are required for VLBI or other specialized observations
Complete source/field list: Sky coordinates, object names, expected flux densities
Required precision: Astrometric accuracy needed (e.g., "±5 arcsec" or "±0.1 arcsec")
Expected properties: Flux density, spectral index, size, polarization (where relevant)
Variability constraints: If targets must be observed within specific time windows (e.g., pulsar phase, orbital phase)
Cadence: For multi-epoch programmes, specify epochs and time intervals
Required image noise (RMS): Target sensitivity in µJy/beam or mJy/beam
Dynamic range: Minimum brightness ratio that must be preserved in images (e.g., "1000:1")
Required data products: Images, cubes, visibility data, catalog extracts, etc.
Required resolution: FWHM of synthesized beam (e.g., "1 arcsec" or "5 milliarcsec")
Flexibility: Whether resolution can be degraded if time constraints require
Channel width: Required frequency resolution (e.g., "1 kHz" or "1 km/s")
Velocity resolution: For kinematic studies, specify required precision
Frequency coverage: Continuous vs. targeted narrow bands
Stokes parameters required: I (total intensity), Q, U, V (linear and circular polarization)
Polarization sensitivity: Required fractional polarization measurement precision (e.g., "1% of point-source flux")

h3: Time Resolution (for transient or pulsar observations)

ul.li: Integration time: For correlator output (e.g., "1 second integration")
Real-time processing: If online algorithms are required (e.g., pulsar dedispersion, RFI filtering)
Proprietary period: Duration during which data is exclusive to the PI (typically 12–18 months)
Data products to archive: Which data products will be preserved in the SKA archive (raw visibilities, calibrated images, catalogs)
Long-term data access: Commitment to making data and results available to the community post-proprietary period
Predicted data rate: Total data volume per observation block (in TB or PB)
Processing capability: Whether the team has infrastructure for rapid data processing or requires SKA compute resources
Real-time reduction: If on-the-fly data reduction is needed (e.g., image production, source extraction)
Sensitive targets: If observations of Earth's location are requested, justification for necessity
Data privacy: Confirmation that no proprietary information from industrial or other parties is being obtained
Dual-use technology: Disclosure if observations could be relevant to surveillance or other sensitive applications
Seasonal restrictions: If targets are only observable during certain seasons (e.g., rising in the southern hemisphere winter only)
Lunar phase requirements: For observations sensitive to moon interference (e.g., observations near strong RFI sources)
Array configuration: If specific extended vs. compact configurations are required
Simultaneous multi-site observations: If SKA-Low and SKA-Mid must observe concurrently
Rapid response urgency: For transient or ToO observations, expected response time (hours, days, weeks)

p: In addition to attributes, every proposal requires:

ul.li: Scientific justification: Detailed explanation of the science question and why it requires SKA observations
Technical feasibility: Discussion of how the requested parameters achieve the science goals
Comparison with existing data: How SKA observations improve on current state of knowledge
Expected impact: Broader implications for the field

ac:rich-text-body.p: The SKA provides a standardized proposal template ensuring all required information is collected. The template includes guidance on filling each section and typical page limits for different proposal types. Use the template provided in the Cycle Call.

p: Before submitting, verify your proposal includes:

ac:task.ac:task-body: Data management plan and proprietary period

ac:rich-text-body.p: Contact the SKA User Support team if you have questions about proposal attributes or technical feasibility. Early contact during proposal preparation is encouraged and can save time during review.
